frameworks.name: fast periodic visual stimulation (FPVS) frequency-tagging
frameworks.one_line: Periodic oddball changes elicit objective EEG response at predicted frequency.

frameworks.name: face inversion effect
frameworks.one_line: Inversion reduces response, indexing high-level face processing.

core_question: How reliably, how fast, and over what duration does the human brain detect brief changes in facial expression?
key_assumptions: Periodic oddball expression changes produce a tagged response at the predicted base-frequency harmonic
Reduction under inversion implies high-level (face-specific) processing
manipulations: Expression of oddball face (fear, disgust, happiness) vs neutral base
Base stimulation rate (5.88 Hz vs 12 Hz) and ratio of expression change
key_findings: A 1.18 Hz response and harmonics indexed expression-change detection in every participant within minutes
Response was largely reduced for inverted faces
Different expression changes evoked reliably distinct topographical maps over occipito-temporal sites
Time-domain responses to expression change occurred between 100 and 310 ms post-change
interpretations: Brief facial expression changes are detected rapidly and then integrated over a sustained period
Partly distinct neural generators support discrimination among expression categories
